incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 2 (works but not optimal)

params.description: Bar graph updates to Scale of 2. All bars land on tick marks but axis has many marks.

steps.text: Scale of 2 does work. All the values land exactly. But look at the ones place digits: 60, 45, 30, 15. They all end in 0 or 5. Which scale would give you fewer lines on the axis?

incorrects.description: Student selected Scale of 5 (works but not optimal)

params.description: Bar graph updates to Scale of 5. All bars land on tick marks.

steps.text: Scale of 5 works. All values land exactly. But the biggest number is 60. That means you need tick marks at 0, 5, 10, 15, all the way to 60. That's 13 marks. Scale of 10 would use fewer.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Bar graph cycles through scales 2, 5, and 10, showing tick mark density for each.

steps.text: Let me show you how to choose. First, I check the biggest number: 60. That's the maximum the graph needs to reach. Next, I look at the ones place digits: 60, 45, 30, 15. All end in 0 or 5, so they're multiples of 5. That means scales of 5 and 10 will both show exact values. Now I compare. Scale of 5 needs 13 tick marks to reach 60. Scale of 10 only needs 7. Same accuracy, cleaner graph. That's why Scale of 10 is the best choice for this data.

and.selected: All values end in 0 or 5 so they can be graphed precisely.

not.selected: The graph is easy to read.
The biggest number is 60 so the bars fit on the graph.
Scale of 2 takes too many marks to reach the biggest number.

incorrects.description: Student selected the precision reason but missed one or more other reasons

steps.text: You found one reason. Now think about the biggest number: 60. Does it fit on the graph without going past the axis? And think about Scale of 2. How many tick marks would it take to reach 60 with that scale?

not.selected: All values end in 0 or 5 so they can be graphed precisely.

or.selected: The graph is easy to read.
The biggest number is 60 so the bars fit on the graph.
Scale of 2 takes too many marks to reach the biggest number.

incorrects.description: Student selected some reasons but missed the precision reason (ending in 0 or 5)

params.description: All four values highlight in data table.

steps.text: Look at the ones place digits: 60, 45, 30, 15. What do you notice? They all end in 0 or 5. That tells you something important about which scales will show exact values.

incorrects.description: Student did not select all four reasons. System models the complete reasoning.

steps.text: Here's how to think through all the reasons. First, the biggest number is 60. The graph fits it without extending the axis. Second, look at the ones digits: 60, 45, 30, 15 all end in 0 or 5, so Scale of 10 can graph them precisely. Third, Scale of 10 only needs 7 tick marks to reach 60. Scale of 2 would need 31 marks. That's a lot. Fourth, the graph is clean and easy to read. All four reasons point to Scale of 10 as the best choice.
